classification.reason: This magnetic parts tray is specifically designed for automotive and workshop use, holding screws, bolts, and metal parts during repairs. The category tags and product claims clearly position it for garage/workshop environments.

buyer_motivation.surface: A magnetic tray to hold small parts during repairs
buyer_motivation.deep: To feel competent and in control during mechanical work without the frustration of lost parts
buyer_motivation.avoidance: The maddening experience of dropping and losing small screws or bolts mid-repair, forcing them to stop work and search
buyer_motivation.identity: Someone who is organized, prepared, and capable of handling their own repairs professionally

modality.reason: The buyer motivation is specifically about the 'maddening experience' of dropping and losing small parts mid-repair. Opening with this exact frustration moment creates immediate emotional connection before revealing the magnetic solution. The high-credibility proof types (live-demonstration, comparison, problem-framing) all work perfectly within this structure, showing the chaos of dropped screws then the magnetic capture in real-time.

rejected.reason: While the magnetic mechanism is visually compelling, jumping straight to the solution misses the critical emotional hook of the frustration experience that drives this purchase decision.

The product's value isn't just visual transformation but emotional relief from a specific pain point. Text overlays can't capture the frustration narrative as effectively as showing the actual problem scenario.

angle.name: The Gravity Defier
angle.conversion_hypothesis: If we open with the tray loaded with heavy sockets tilted completely vertical while parts stay in place, then the viewer will believe the magnet is genuinely strong enough for their heaviest work, which makes buying it feel like solving their parts-dropping problem
angle.entry_mechanism: Open mid-action with the creator tilting a fully-loaded magnetic tray to 90 degrees vertical against a car fender, heavy sockets and bolts clearly visible staying in place
angle.proof_vehicle: Live demonstration of the 90-degree tilt test with actual socket sets and heavy bolts, followed by placing the loaded tray on vertical surfaces
angle.belief_formation_path: Viewer sees impossible-seeming physics (heavy parts defying gravity) → realizes this isn't a weak magnet → pictures their own dropped screws problem solved → wants the peace of mind
angle.cta_setup: After seeing multiple gravity-defying demonstrations, viewer is convinced of strength and imagining their own frustration solved
angle.creator_pattern_source: @dealswithty pattern - disbelief-reveal hook with live-demonstration proof that stacks multiple impressive capabilities

beats.content: Creator loads magnetic tray with heavy socket set pieces and large bolts, then tilts the entire tray completely vertical against a car fender - all parts stay magnetically attached
beats.spoken: Watch what happens when I tilt this thing completely sideways
beats.filming_note: Start with tray horizontal, load it with visibly heavy sockets and bolts, then in one smooth motion tilt to 90 degrees vertical against car. Hold for 2 seconds to let viewer process

beats.content: Cut to scattered screws and small bolts across a messy workbench, then creator sweeps them all onto the magnetic tray in one motion - they all stick instantly
beats.spoken: I used to lose screws under the car every single time I worked on something
beats.filming_note: Show real mess of small parts on workbench, then use tray like a magnet wand to collect them all in one satisfying sweep

beats.content: Side-by-side comparison: regular bowl filled with same parts tilted sideways (parts fall out) next to magnetic tray tilted same angle (parts stay put)
beats.spoken: This thing has saved me probably twenty trips back to the hardware store
beats.filming_note: Film both containers side by side, tilt them simultaneously to same angle, let the contrast speak for itself

beats.content: Creator places loaded magnetic tray on vertical toolbox side, then car door frame, showing it sticks and holds position with all parts intact
beats.spoken: Obviously this won't hold your entire toolbox, but for the parts you're actually working with
beats.filming_note: Show unexpected vertical surface mounting - toolbox side, car door, any metal vertical surface where it sticks and stays

beats.content: Close-up of rubber base sliding across painted car hood surface, then wider shot showing no scratches or marks left behind
beats.spoken: I've been using this for about six months now on every project
beats.filming_note: Start tight on rubber base contact with painted surface, slide it around, then pull back to show clean surface with no damage

beats.content: Final shot of creator working under car hood with magnetic tray stuck to fender, easily grabbing bolts as needed without looking down
beats.spoken: TikTok Shop has it - nine ninety-nine to never crawl around looking for dropped bolts again
beats.filming_note: Show natural working scenario where tray is positioned for easy access while hands are busy with actual repair work

hook_options.hook_text: Watch what happens when I tilt this thing completely sideways
hook_options.rationale: Opens mid-demonstration with impossible-seeming physics about to be proven. The word 'sideways' understates what's actually vertical, creating curiosity gap about magnet strength
hook_options.performance_note: Start loading the tray while speaking, then execute the tilt as you finish the sentence. Let the visual proof land before any explanation

hook_options.hook_text: I dropped my third socket this month under the car
hook_options.rationale: Specific relatable frustration that every DIY person has experienced. The number 'third' makes it feel real and current, not hypothetical
hook_options.performance_note: Say this while showing scattered parts on workbench, frustrated tone but not overdramatic. Immediately transition to gathering them with the magnetic tray

hook_options.hook_text: Heavy sockets and bolts stuck vertically to a car fender, defying gravity
hook_options.rationale: Pure visual disbelief hook - viewer sees something that shouldn't be possible and needs to understand how. No setup needed, just impossible physics
hook_options.performance_note: Open with the tray already vertical and loaded, hold the shot for 2 full seconds in silence to let the impossibility register, then explain

problem_solution_logic.problem_framing: The maddening cycle of dropping small parts during repairs and having to stop work to search for them, often losing expensive sockets or critical bolts
problem_solution_logic.why_this_framing: This is the exact frustration that drives tool purchases - not organization for its own sake, but the workflow interruption and cost of lost parts that every DIYer has experienced
problem_solution_logic.solution_positioning: A magnetic capture system that eliminates the dropping problem entirely by holding parts against gravity, even in awkward positions

cta_logic.viewer_state_at_cta: Convinced the magnet is genuinely strong after seeing multiple gravity-defying demonstrations, picturing their own dropped parts frustration solved
cta_logic.cta_text: TikTok Shop has it - nine ninety-nine to never crawl around looking for dropped bolts again
cta_logic.why_it_works: Callbacks to the core problem (crawling around looking for dropped parts) while positioning the purchase as buying peace of mind, not just a tool. Price feels reasonable for the frustration it eliminates

script.caption: this magnetic tray actually holds heavy sockets when you tilt it vertical. been using it for months and haven't lost a single bolt since. the rubber base doesn't scratch paint either which is huge when you're working on the car
script.content_structure_note: Opens with impossible physics demonstration to create immediate disbelief, then works backward to show the problem this solves. Each proof escalation shows the magnet working in increasingly challenging conditions, building credibility through visual evidence rather than claims.

filming_instructions.location: Garage or driveway with car and workbench visible

shots.label: Opening tilt test
instructions: Load tray with heavy sockets and bolts while speaking
Tilt to full 90 degrees against car fender in smooth motion
Hold vertical position for 2 seconds to show parts staying put

shots.label: Problem demonstration
instructions: Scatter various small parts across workbench surface
Use magnetic tray to sweep them up in one satisfying motion
Show all parts sticking to tray surface

shots.label: Comparison test
instructions: Fill regular bowl with same parts as magnetic tray
Position both containers side by side
Tilt both simultaneously to same angle
Let visual contrast show the difference

shots.label: Vertical mounting
instructions: Place loaded tray on toolbox side or car door frame
Show it sticking and holding position
Demonstrate accessing parts while tray is mounted vertically

shots.label: Surface protection
instructions: Close-up of rubber base on painted car surface
Slide tray around to show no scratching
Pull back to show clean surface with no marks

shots.label: Real use scenario
instructions: Position tray on car fender while working under hood
Show natural workflow of grabbing parts without looking down
Demonstrate hands-free access during actual repair work

filming_instructions.lighting: Natural outdoor lighting or bright garage lighting to clearly show parts and surfaces
filming_instructions.equipment_note: Phone camera sufficient, focus on steady shots that clearly show parts staying attached to magnet during tilting

flags.issue: CTA mentions price but could be stronger by leading with the proof callback about never losing bolts again
Side-by-side comparison shot at 8s-13s may be challenging for solo creator to execute smoothly
